Retained ownership, multiple use. This is pretty important, real important for copywriters. It's very important for anybody who's creating kind of work product is that, so if you create this whole system for hiring better people for your client in the electrical manufacturing industry, you want to retain the rights to sell that system to everybody, every place except electrical manufacturing. So now you've built a product. If you're dealing with a geographically restricted client, so let's say you create some kind of a system for a group of restaurants in Texas, you want to retain the rights to sell that same system to restaurants in every other state, but Texas. So you now get to bicycle the stuff across the country and resell it and resell it and resell it and resell it. Money from referred vendors, almost always when you do consulting work for somebody, you're gonna need to connect them with goods and services you don't provide, you should get paid. Your choice whether you disclose it or not, but you should get paid. Resale of goods and services, meaning not only do they hire you as a consultant, but then they need a whole library of your stuff. And so you sell product at the same time. Now let's talk about other compensation structures. There's performance dollars and there's use fees. Performance dollars, one structure is, I talked about it a couple times quickly and casually, is percentage above base. Percentage above base is a very easy thing to sell and a very difficult thing to retain clients with over a long period of time. So it has a positive and a negative. Percentage above base works like, the very simple example is okay, you made 100 grand this year, has a mortgage broker and there's a rate of inflation at 10% and your growth each year is 10%. So your base this year is 120,000 and your base next year would be 136,000 adding the 20% to this year's base. And so you figure out the base for each one of the years and then you take a percentage of everything that occurs above the base. So now if I can get him when he's at 100 and I can take him to 300 and his base was 120, I had 280 of which I'm getting 30% of. And ideal I'm getting it above some kind of a fee, not in lieu of a fee. There can be very big money made with percentage above base. The reversal of it is where you're saving people money. So I'm gonna come in and find 52 ways to reduce costs in your factory and I'm gonna get some agreed upon percentage of the reduction. We had a percentage above base thing in Jack Henry's business in theft control with a few large supermarket chains where two year contract, we establish your base theft rate and every half point we reduce it by, we get X amount of dollars and these are big dollars. Now the negative of percentage above base is it usually creates an adversarial relationship that ultimately breeds resentment. Because in most cases the work you do for them is front and loaded. The results get better and better over time. So they are paying you more and more for less and less. And although they should be happy, at some point as they are getting out the checkbook to write the check this month for $52,000 and they haven't even talked to you in 90 days, they're grumpy. And so the first thing you ought to know about percentage above base is the longer the contract, the less likely you are not to have problems and to get paid. The one way to attempt to deal with that is with buyouts. So that there's a way for them at certain trigger points to cut you a lump sum check and terminate the relationship and continue to use everything they got during the point. Now you can sell against percentage above base for pre-pace, which is what we did in practice management. So we signed the doctor to the three year, 30% percentage above base contract and now we convince him that he's gonna wind up paying us $412,000 over the next three years. But you can prepay your way out for 30. Well, and we were doing this, the interest rates, this should be Carter, I think, the interest rates were sky high on a bank loan.
